title: Excess nitrogen from croplands
subtitle: "Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.
caption:
Excess nitrogen from croplands, 2009
"Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.

This choropleth map displays the total mass of excess nitrogen originating from croplands across the globe for the year 2009. The visualization employs a continuous color scale to represent the quantity of nitrogen lost to the environment, ranging from light yellow for lower amounts to deep reddish-brown for the highest concentrations. The legend defines specific thresholds, starting at 100,000 tonnes and ascending through 250,000 tonnes, 1 million tonnes, 10 million tonnes, and finally 25 million tonnes. Areas shaded in the darkest brown, indicating excess nitrogen exceeding 25 million tonnes, are visible in China and India, marking them as the regions with the largest surplus. The United States and Brazil appear in a slightly lighter but still intense orange-brown shade, placing them in the band between 10 million and 25 million tonnes. Western and Central Europe, including France and Germany, along with parts of Southeast Asia like Indonesia, show values in the 1 million to 10 million tonne range. Lighter yellow and orange hues cover much of the rest of the agricultural land in South America, Eastern Europe, and scattered regions in Africa and Asia, corresponding to values between 100,000 and 1 million tonnes. Large portions of the map, including Canada, Russia, Australia, and most of the African continent, are colored in pale blue or white, signifying either nitrogen mining or values below the 100,000 tonne threshold. Greenland and parts of the Sahara are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data. The geographic distribution shows the highest absolute volumes of excess nitrogen concentrated in East and South Asia, with significant secondary clusters in North America and South America.
